title: Arrays & Loops
description: Vectors are C++'s workhorse container for interviews — master declaration, access, push_back, and both classic and range-based for loops to handle any array problem.

sections.title: Vector declaration and access
sections.explanation: Use **vector<int>** instead of raw arrays — interviewers expect it. Declare with a size, with initial values, or empty and grow with push_back. Access elements with `[]` (no bounds checking) or `.at()` (throws on out-of-bounds). Use `.size()` to get the length.
sections.example:
vector<int> nums = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
cout << nums[0] << endl;      // 10
cout << nums.size() << endl;  // 5
cout << nums.back() << endl;  // 50

vector<int> zeros(5, 0);  // [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
cout << zeros.size() << endl; // 5

exercise.prompt: Create a vector<int> with values {5, 10, 15, 20}. Print the first element, the last element (using .back()), and the size.

validation.message: Print the first element (index 0), last element (.back()), and size
Use [0] for first, .back() for last, .size() for length

exercise.hint: nums[0] gives the first element, nums.back() gives the last, nums.size() gives the count.

sections.title: push_back and classic for loop
sections.explanation: Build vectors dynamically with **push_back()**. The classic for loop `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)` gives you index access, which you need for problems involving adjacent elements, two pointers, or index-based logic.

exercise.prompt: Create an empty vector and use push_back to add the squares of 1 through 5 (1, 4, 9, 16, 25). Then print each element separated by spaces using a classic for loop.

validation.message: Push squares of 1-5 and print them space-separated
Use push_back to build the vector and index access to print

exercise.hint: squares.push_back(i * i) inside the first loop. Access with squares[i] in the second loop.

sections.title: Range-based for loop
sections.explanation: When you don't need the index, use a **range-based for loop**: `for (int x : nums)`. Use `for (int& x : nums)` if you need to modify elements in place. This is cleaner and less error-prone, which interviewers appreciate.

exercise.prompt: Given a vector {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, use a range-based for loop with a reference to double each element in place. Then use another range-based for loop to print the results space-separated.

validation.message: Use int& for modification and int (by value) for reading

exercise.hint: Use x *= 2 to double each element. The reference int& x allows modifying the original vector.

sections.title: 2D vectors (grids)
sections.explanation: Many interview problems involve grids or matrices. Declare a 2D vector with `vector<vector<int>>`. Initialize with a size using `vector<vector<int>>(rows, vector<int>(cols, val))`. Access with `grid[row][col]`.

exercise.prompt: Create a 3x3 identity matrix using a 2D vector (1s on the diagonal, 0s elsewhere). Print it row by row with spaces between elements.

validation.message: The identity matrix has 1s on the diagonal and 0s elsewhere
Use vector<vector<int>> for the 2D grid and grid[i][i] for the diagonal

exercise.hint: Set grid[i][i] = 1 for each row i. The diagonal of a matrix is where row index equals column index.
